risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "In this randomized open label pilot study" and "After baseline measurements and randomization to either 1,200 or 2,400 mg of NAC per day" Comment: The study states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe the method used to generate the allocation sequence (e.g. computer-generated numbers, random number table, blocks, or similar). Per the review rules, "randomized" without further context is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, so the risk remains unclear.

_evidence.method_quote: "In this randomized open label pilot study, the effects of oral NAC treatment on markers of oxidative stress and hemolysis in sickle cell patients were investigated." "After baseline measurements and randomization to either 1,200 or 2,400 mg of NAC per day, patients started taking NAC ..."
_evidence.method_summary: Patients were randomized to 1,200 mg or 2,400 mg NAC per day; the article does not describe how the sequence was generated.
context_clues: randomization to either 1,200 or 2,400 mg of NAC per day

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article reports that patients were randomized, but it gives no information about how the allocation sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants. The only relevant wording is: "After baseline measurements and randomization to either 1,200 or 2,400 mg of NAC per day," and the extracted evidence states the concealment mechanism was "not described." Comment: Because no explicit concealment method is described (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, or sequentially numbered identical containers), the recruiter could not be shown to have been prevented from foreseeing assignments, so the correct judgement is Unclear risk.

_evidence.note: The article states only: "After baseline measurements and randomization to either 1,200 or 2,400 mg of NAC per day" and describes this as an "open label pilot study," but it does not describe any allocation concealment method.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "In this randomized open label pilot study, the effects of oral NAC treatment on markers of oxidative stress and hemolysis in sickle cell patients were investigated." Comment: The study explicitly states it was open label, so participants and personnel were not blinded. This could plausibly affect subjective outcomes reported here (e.g. daily pain score, adverse events, hospital admission due to SCD-related complications), so the dominant risk for this domain is high.

_evidence.blinding_quote: In this randomized open label pilot study, the effects of oral NAC treatment on markers of oxidative stress and hemolysis in sickle cell patients were investigated.
outcome_categories: hospital admission due to SCD related complications
subjective_outcomes: hospital admission due to SCD related complications

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients kept a daily pain score diary (visual analogue scale pain score)." and "During the treatment period, none of the study patients was admitted with SCD related complications. The daily pain score did not change during treatment (data not shown)." Comment: The study assessed at least one patient-reported subjective outcome (daily pain score) and side effects/adverse events via questionnaires, while no explicit blinding of participants or outcome assessors is reported. Because the patient is the assessor for self-reported pain and participants were in an open-label study, knowledge of allocation could influence measurement of these subjective outcomes; by the worst-outcome rule, this makes the domain high risk even though the biochemical outcomes were objective.

outcomes.label: markers of hemolysis (hemoglobin, reticulocytes, LDH, bilirubin)
daily pain score diary (visual analogue scale pain score)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Eleven patients ... were included in the study. One patient (P4) discontinued using NAC after 3 weeks and withdrew from the study." and Table 1 shows "1,200 mg (n = 6)" and "2,400 mg (n = 4)" at follow-up. Comment: The analysed outcome data are nearly complete: 10/11 randomized participants were analysed overall (total loss 9.1%), with no loss in the 1,200 mg arm (6/6 analysed) and one withdrawal in the 2,400 mg arm (5 randomized, 4 analysed). Although the per-arm loss is imbalanced in percentage terms, the absolute amount of attrition is very small and the paper reports a specific non-outcome-related reason (withdrew from the study), so this is unlikely to have biased the outcome results.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “One patient (P4) discontinued using NAC after 3 weeks and withdrew from the study.”

_evidence.notes: Per-arm numbers are taken from Table 1 (“1,200 mg (n = 6)”, “2,400 mg (n = 4)”) plus the Results text noting one withdrawal. No CONSORT flow diagram was provided.
